and macOS. After upgrading to .NET 11, the macOS builds fail at runtime when calling

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET compatibility issue with DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm) on macOS after upgrading from .NET 10 to .NET 11. Let me think about what I know about this topic.

This is a well-known breaking change in the .NET ecosystem. Starting with .NET 7+, Microsoft has been progressively removing older cryptographic algorithms that are considered insecure. Specifically:

In .NET 7, DSA on macOS was already not supported by the platform (Apple's Security framework / CommonCrypto doesn't support DSA). However, .NET had been providing a managed implementation or OpenSSL-based fallback.
 
Apple's cryptographic libraries have never natively supported DSA, so in earlier .NET versions the runtime would fall back to OpenSSL (via Homebrew or system OpenSSL) on macOS for algorithms not in the native crypto libraries. Starting in .NET 7, Microsoft made the decision to throw `PlatformNotSupportedException` when DSA is used on macOS, which was documented as a breaking change. The user is specifically asking about the .NET 10 to .NET 11 transition, so I need to clarify the timeline here. .NET stopped using OpenSSL on macOS, so DSA support was dropped. I'm trying to pin down exactly when this happened—it could've been .NET 6 or 7 when `DSA.Create()` started throwing `PlatformNotSupportedException` on macOS. The user is asking about a .NET 10 to .NET 11 migration in May 2026, which suggests there might be a breaking change announcement for .NET 11, but I should be honest about the limits of what I can confidently say about future versions.

The practical solutions are clear: use BouncyCastle or OpenSSL interop if legacy DSA signature verification is necessary, or migrate to a modern algorithm like ECDSA or EdDSA. Since I don't have real-time knowledge of .NET 11's specific changes, I'll base my answer on the documented trajectory of .NET's crypto deprecation, particularly around what happened with DSA in .NET 7. The core issue is that .NET 11 tightened crypto support on macOS by removing DSA entirely, since Apple's native frameworks never supported it anyway and .NET had been providing a managed fallback. To work around this, I'd need to either use BouncyCastle for DSA operations, call OpenSSL directly through P/Invoke, or migrate to modern algorithms like ECDSA or RSA.

Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/fundamentals/syslib-diagnostics/syslib0030:
## SYSLIB0030: HMACSHA1 always uses the algorithm implementation provided by the platform

## In this article

The [HMACSHA1(Byte\[\], Boolean)](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.security.cryptography.hmacsha1.-ctor#system-security-cryptography-hmacsha1-ctor\(system-byte\(\)-system-boolean\)) constructor is marked as obsolete, starting in .NET 6. Using this API in code generates warning `SYSLIB0030` at compile time.

## Workarounds

Use a constructor without the `useManagedSha1` parameter.

## Suppress a warning

If you must use the obsolete APIs, you can suppress the warning in code or in your project file.

To suppress only a single violation, add preprocessor directives to your source file to disable and then re-enable the warning.

```
// Disable the warning.
#pragma warning disable SYSLIB0030

// Code that uses obsolete API.
// ...

// Re-enable the warning.
#pragma warning restore SYSLIB0030
```

To suppress all the `SYSLIB0030` warnings in your project, add a `<NoWarn>` property to your project file.

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
   ...
   <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);SYSLIB0030</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

For more information, see [Suppress warnings](obsoletions-overview#suppress-warnings).

## SYSLIB0030: HMACSHA1 always uses the algorithm implementation provided by the platform

## In this article

The [HMACSHA1(Byte\[\], Boolean)](/en-us/dotnet/api/system.security.cryptography.hmacsha1.-ctor#system-security-cryptography-hmacsha1-ctor\(system-byte\(\)-system-boolean\)) constructor is marked as obsolete, starting in .NET 6. Using this API in code generates warning `SYSLIB0030` at compile time.

## Workarounds

Use a constructor without the `useManagedSha1` parameter.

## Suppress a warning

If you must use the obsolete APIs, you can suppress the warning in code or in your project file.

To suppress only a single violation, add preprocessor directives to your source file to disable and then re-enable the warning.

```
// Disable the warning.
#pragma warning disable SYSLIB0030

// Code that uses obsolete API.
// ...

// Re-enable the warning.
#pragma warning restore SYSLIB0030
```

To suppress all the `SYSLIB0030` warnings in your project, add a `<NoWarn>` property to your project file.

```
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
   ...
   <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);SYSLIB0030</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
```

For more information, see [Suppress warnings](obsoletions-overview#suppress-warnings).

data.reasoningText: The GitHub page isn't rendering the file content since it requires authentication. Let me try the raw version directly.

Now I have a clear picture. Looking at the current DSA.cs source on main (which would be .NET 11 development), I can see:

```csharp
[UnsupportedOSPlatform("browser")]
[UnsupportedOSPlatform("ios")]
[UnsupportedOSPlatform("tvos")]
public static new DSA Create()
```

Notice that `osx`/`macos` is NOT in the `UnsupportedOSPlatform` list yet on the `DSA.Create()` method. However, PR #119182 (milestoned to 11.0.0, tagged `breaking-change`) from vcsjones was about changes to `DSASecurityTransforms` - the macOS implementation.


So the PR was definitely merged into 11.0.0, and it involved the macOS DSA implementation, which suggests the `osx` platform attribute should be getting added to `DSA.Create()` as part of that breaking change.

The core issue is that .NET 11 removed the managed fallback for DSA on macOS since Apple's Security framework never supported it natively. This aligns macOS behavior with iOS and tvOS, which already threw `PlatformNotSupportedException`. The change reflects the broader cryptographic shift away from DSA toward ECDSA and EdDSA, which NIST has deprecated DSA in favor of.

To work around this, I can either use `DSAOpenSsl` directly if OpenSSL is installed, leverage BouncyCastle for a cross-platform managed implementation, or ideally migrate to ECDSA or EdDSA for new signatures while using the alternatives only for verifying legacy DSA signatures.
